tEn: Elon Musk on War, Human Nature, and Peace in the Middle East
tZh: 埃隆·马斯克谈战争、人性与中东和平
sEn: Elon Musk discusses whether war is inherent to human nature or a product of societal structures, and shares his controversial views on achieving peace in the Israel-Gaza conflict through conspicuous acts of kindness.
sZh: 埃隆·马斯克探讨战争是人性固有还是社会结构产物，并就以色列-加沙冲突提出通过显著善行实现和平的争议性观点。

consensus.en: War is part of human nature, but intelligence can help control violent instincts.
consensus.zh: 战争是人性的一部分，但智能有助于控制暴力本能。

consensus.en: Conspicuous acts of kindness can break cycles of violence and reduce terrorism.
consensus.zh: 显著的善行可以打破暴力循环，减少恐怖主义。

consensus.en: Becoming a multiplanetary civilization is essential to avoid extinction from natural or man-made disasters.
consensus.zh: 成为多行星文明对于避免因自然或人为灾难而灭绝至关重要。

consensus.en: AI safety requires regulatory oversight and objective third-party referees.
consensus.zh: AI安全需要监管监督和客观的第三方裁判。

consensus.en: Tesla's real-world AI approach is more compute-efficient than large language models.
consensus.zh: 特斯拉的真实世界AI方法比大型语言模型更高效。

consensus.en: Community Notes on X work because people who historically disagree must agree for a note to be shown.
consensus.zh: X上的社区笔记之所以有效，是因为历史上意见不同的人必须达成一致才能显示笔记。

contrarian.en: Hamas wanted to provoke an overreaction from Israel; Israel should respond with conspicuous kindness.
contrarian.zh: 哈马斯意图激怒以色列过度反应；以色列应以显著善行回应。

contrarian.en: Eternal peace might be worse than limited war, as it could suppress progress and create a sterile society.
contrarian.zh: 永久和平可能比有限战争更糟，因为它会压制进步，创造僵化的社会。

contrarian.en: The simulation hypothesis implies the creators don't know the outcome, supporting free will.
contrarian.zh: 模拟假说意味着创造者不知道结果，支持自由意志。

contrarian.en: China is not inquisitive historically; it focuses internally, which is positive for avoiding conflict.
contrarian.zh: 中国历史上不具侵略性；它专注于内部，这对避免冲突是积极的。

contrarian.en: The SEC's failure to pursue hedge funds shorting Tesla shows regulatory capture.
contrarian.zh: SEC未能追查做空特斯拉的对冲基金，显示了监管俘获。

contrarian.en: Cynicism is a red flag; most people are medium good, and cynics excuse their own bad behavior.
contrarian.zh: 犬儒主义是危险信号；大多数人中等善良，犬儒者为自己的不良行为找借口。

turns.en: The following is a conversation with Elon Musk, his fourth time on The Lex Fridman podcast. I thought you were going to finish it. It's one of the greatest themes in all film history. Yeah, that's great. So I was just thinking about the Roman Empire, as one does. There's that whole meme where all guys are thinking about the Roman Empire at least once a day, and half the population is confused whether it's true or not. But more seriously, thinking about the wars going on in the world today, and as you know, war and military conquest has been a big part of Roman society and culture, and I think it has been a big part of most empires and dynasties throughout human history. So yeah, they usually came as a result of conquest. I mean, there's some like the Austro-Hungarian Empire where there was just a lot of clever marriages, but fundamentally there's an engine of conquest, and they celebrate excellence in warfare. Many of the leaders were excellent generals, yeah, that kind of thing. So big picture question: Grok approved? I asked this is a good question to ask. Tested Grok approved, at least on fun mode. To what degree do you think war is part of human nature versus a consequence of how human societies are structured? I asked this as you have somehow controversially been a proponent of peace.
turns.zh: 以下是与埃隆·马斯克的对话，这是他第四次上《莱克斯·弗里德曼播客》。我以为你会把它弹完。这是电影史上最伟大的主题之一。是的，很棒。所以我刚才在想罗马帝国，就像人们常做的那样。有个梗说所有男人每天至少想一次罗马帝国，而一半的人困惑这是不是真的。但更严肃地说，想想当今世界的战争，如你所知，战争和军事征服一直是罗马社会和文化的重要组成部分，我认为这也是人类历史上大多数帝国和王朝的重要组成部分。是的，它们通常源于征服。有些像奥匈帝国那样，有很多巧妙的联姻，但根本上还是征服的引擎，他们崇尚战争中的卓越。许多领袖都是优秀的将军，是的，诸如此类。所以一个大问题：Grok 批准了吗？我问了这个问题，是个好问题。测试过，Grok 批准了，至少在趣味模式下。你认为战争在多大程度上是人性的一部分，而不是人类社会结构的结果？我问这个是因为你不知为何有争议地成了和平的支持者。

turns.en: I'm generally a proponent of peace. I mean, ignorance is perhaps in my view the real enemy to be countered. That's the real hard part, not fighting other humans. But all creatures fight. I mean, the jungle—you look at people think of nature as perhaps some sort of peaceful thing, but in fact it is not. There's some quite funny W. W. H. thing where he's like in the jungle, saying that it's like basically just murder and death in every direction. I mean, the plants and animals in the jungle are constantly trying to kill each other every single day, every minute. So it's not like we're unusual in that respect.
turns.zh: 我总体上支持和平。我的意思是，在我看来，无知才是真正需要对抗的敌人。那才是真正的难点，而不是与其他人战斗。但所有生物都会争斗。我的意思是，丛林——人们认为大自然也许是某种和平的东西，但实际上并非如此。有个挺搞笑的 W. W. H. 的东西，他说在丛林里，基本上四面八方都是谋杀和死亡。我是说，丛林里的植物和动物每天都在每分每秒地试图互相残杀。所以在这方面我们并不特别。

turns.en: Well, there's a relevant question here: whether with greater intelligence comes greater control over these base instincts for violence.
turns.zh: 嗯，这里有个相关的问题：更高的智能是否带来对这些暴力本能的更强控制。

turns.en: Yes, we have much more ability to control our limbic instinct for violence than say a chimpanzee. And in fact, if one looks at chimpanzee society, it is not friendly. I mean, the bonobos are an exception, but chimpanzee society is filled with violence and it's quite horrific frankly. That's the limbic system in action. You don't want to be on the wrong side of a chimpanzee; he'll eat your face off and tear your nuts off. Yeah, basically there's no limits or ethics. The Romans said 'just war'; there's no just war in chimpanzee societies. It's war and dominance by any means necessary. Chimpanzee society is like a primitive version of human society. They're not peace-loving at all. There's extreme violence. And then once in a while, somebody who's watched too many Disney movies decides to raise a chimpanzee as a pet, and then that eats their face or tears their nuts off or chews their fingers off. That kind of thing. It's happened several times.
turns.zh: 是的，我们比黑猩猩有更强的控制暴力本能的能力。事实上，如果你看看黑猩猩社会，它并不友好。我的意思是，倭黑猩猩是个例外，但黑猩猩社会充满了暴力，坦率说非常可怕。那是边缘系统在起作用。你不想站在黑猩猩的对立面；它会吃掉你的脸，扯掉你的蛋。是的，基本上没有限制或道德。罗马人讲“正义战争”；黑猩猩社会没有正义战争。那是不择手段的战争和统治。黑猩猩社会是人类社会的原始版本。它们根本不爱好和平。有极端的暴力。然后偶尔有人看了太多迪士尼电影，决定养一只黑猩猩当宠物，然后那只黑猩猩就吃掉他们的脸，或者扯掉他们的蛋，或者咬掉他们的手指。这种事发生过好几次。

turns.en: Ripping your nuts off is an interesting strategy for interaction. It's happened to people. It's unfortunate. That's I guess one way to ensure that the other chimp doesn't contribute to the gene pool. Well, from a martial arts perspective, it's a fascinating strategy. I wonder which of the martial arts teaches that. I think it's safe to say if somebody's got your nuts in their hand and has the option of ripping them off, you will be amenable to whatever they want. Yeah, so to say. So like I said, somehow controversially, you've been a proponent of peace on Twitter, on X. So let me ask you about the wars going on today and to see what the path to peace could be. How do you hope the current war in Israel and Gaza comes to an end? What path do you see that can minimize human suffering in the long term in that part of the world?
turns.zh: 扯掉你的蛋是一种有趣的互动策略。这发生在人身上过。很不幸。我想这是确保另一只黑猩猩不贡献基因库的一种方式。嗯，从武术角度看，这是一种迷人的策略。我想知道哪种武术教这个。可以安全地说，如果有人抓住了你的蛋，并且可以选择扯掉它们，你会顺从他们想要的任何东西。是的，可以这么说。所以就像我说的，不知为何有争议地，你在 Twitter 和 X 上一直是和平的支持者。那么让我问问你关于当今的战争，以及和平之路可能是什么。你希望当前以色列和加沙的战争如何结束？你看到什么路径可以长期最小化那个地区的人类苦难？

turns.en: Well, I think that part of the world is definitely like if you look up 'there is no easy answer' in the dictionary, it'll be a picture of the Middle East, in Israel especially. So there is no easy answer. What is strictly my opinion is that the goal of Hamas was to provoke an overreaction from Israel. They obviously did not expect to have a military victory, but they really wanted to commit the worst atrocities they could in order to provoke the most aggressive response possible from Israel, and then leverage that aggressive response to rally Muslims worldwide for the cause of Gaza and Palestine, which they have succeeded in doing. So the counterintuitive thing here is that I think the thing that should be done, even though it is very difficult, is that I would recommend that Israel engage in the most conspicuous acts of kindness possible. Everything that would thwart the goal of Hamas. In some sense, to the degree that makes sense in geopolitics, turn the other cheek implemented. It's not exactly turn the other cheek, because I do think it is appropriate for Israel to find the Hamas members and either kill them or incarcerate them. Something has to be done because they're just going to keep coming otherwise. But in addition to that, they need to do whatever they can. There's some talk of establishing a mobile hospital; I'd recommend doing that. Just making sure that there is food, water, medical necessities, and just be over the top about it and be very transparent, so that people can't claim it's a trick. Just put a webcam on the thing, 24/7. Deploy acts of kindness. Conspicuous acts of kindness that are unequivocal, meaning they can't be somehow—because Hamas will then say it's a trick, so you have to counter that. This ultimately fights the broader force of hatred in the region. Yes. And I'm not sure who said it, it's an apocryphal saying, but 'an eye for an eye makes everyone blind.' Now in that neck of the woods, they really believe in the whole eye for an eye thing. But if you're not going to just outright commit genocide against an entire people, which obviously would not be acceptable to anyone, then you're going to leave basically a lot of people alive who subsequently hate Israel. So really the question is: for every Hamas member that you kill, how many did you create? And if you create more than you killed, you've not succeeded. That's the real situation there.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为那个地区绝对是——如果你在字典里查“没有简单答案”，它会是一张中东的图片，尤其以色列。所以没有简单答案。严格来说我的看法是，哈马斯的目标是激怒以色列过度反应。他们显然不指望军事胜利，而是想尽可能犯下最严重的暴行，以引发以色列最激进的回应，然后利用那种激进回应来动员全世界的穆斯林支持加沙和巴勒斯坦的事业，他们成功了。所以这里反直觉的是，我认为应该做的事情，尽管非常困难，是建议以色列进行尽可能引人注目的善举。一切会挫败哈马斯目标的事情。在某种意义上，在符合地缘政治逻辑的程度上，实行“转过另一边脸”。不完全是转过另一边脸，因为我确实认为以色列找到哈马斯成员并杀死或监禁他们是合适的。必须做点什么，否则他们会不断再来。但除此之外，他们需要尽一切可能。有讨论建立移动医院；我建议这么做。确保有食物、水、医疗必需品，并且做得过头一点，非常透明，这样人们就不能说这是个诡计。放个网络摄像头，24/7。部署善举。明确无误的引人注目的善举，意思是它们不能以某种方式——因为哈马斯会说这是个诡计，所以你必须反驳。这最终会对抗该地区更广泛的仇恨力量。是的。我不确定是谁说的，这是个伪托的谚语，但“以眼还眼使所有人盲目”。在那个地区，他们真的相信以眼还眼那一套。但如果你不打算直接对整个人群实施种族灭绝——这显然对任何人都是不可接受的——那么你基本上会留下很多活着的人，他们随后会仇恨以色列。所以真正的问题是：每杀死一个哈马斯成员，你创造了多少个？如果你创造的比杀死的多，你就没有成功。这就是那里的真实情况。

turns.en: You kill somebody's child in Gaza, you've made at least a few Hamas members who will die just to kill an Israeli. That's the situation. But this is one of the most contentious subjects one could possibly discuss. But if the ultimate goal is some sort of long-term peace, one has to look at this from the standpoint of over time, are there more or fewer terrorists being created?
turns.zh: 你在加沙杀了别人的孩子，你就制造了至少几个哈马斯成员，他们会不惜牺牲生命去杀一个以色列人。情况就是这样。但这是最可能引发争议的话题之一。但如果最终目标是某种长期和平，就必须从长远来看，随着时间的推移，是产生了更多还是更少的恐怖分子？

turns.en: Let me just linger on war. Well, it's safe to say wars always existed and always will exist.
turns.zh: 让我就停留在战争这个话题上。可以说，战争一直存在，也永远会存在。

turns.en: Always will exist? I hope not.
turns.zh: 永远会存在？我希望不会。

turns.en: I think there will always be war. The question is just how much war, and the scope and scale of war. But to imagine that there would not be any war in the future, I think would be a very unlikely outcome.
turns.zh: 我认为战争永远存在。问题只是战争的多少，以及战争的规模和范围。但想象未来没有任何战争，我认为是非常不可能的结果。

turns.en: You talked about the Culture series; there's war even there. Yes, a giant war. The first book starts off with a gigantic galactic war where trillions die. But it still protects these pockets of flourishing. Somehow you can have galactic war and still have pockets of flourishing.
turns.zh: 你提到过《文明》系列；那里也有战争。是的，一场巨大的战争。第一本书就以一场巨大的银河战争开场，数万亿人死亡。但它仍然保护着那些繁荣的角落。不知怎的，你可以有银河战争，同时仍有繁荣的角落。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, it's a race between the scale of suffering and the scale of flourishing.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，这是苦难规模与繁荣规模之间的竞赛。

turns.en: A lot of people seem to be using this tragedy to beat the drums of war and feed the military-industrial complex. Do you worry about this? The people who are rooting for escalation, and how can it be stopped?
turns.zh: 很多人似乎正在利用这场悲剧来敲响战鼓，滋养军工复合体。你担心这个吗？那些支持局势升级的人，怎样才能阻止他们？

turns.en: One of the things that does concern me is that there are very few people alive today who actually viscerally understand the horrors of war, at least in the US. I mean, obviously the people on the front lines in Ukraine and Russia understand just how terrible war is. But how many people in the West understand it? My grandfather was in World War II. He was severely traumatized. He was there for almost six years in North Africa and Italy. All his friends were killed in front of him, and he would have died too, except they randomly gave some IQ tests or something, and he scored very high. He was not an officer; he was a corporal or a sergeant because he didn't finish high school. He had to drop out because his dad died, and he had to work to support his siblings. So because he didn't graduate high school, he was not eligible for the officer corps. He got put into the cannon fodder category basically. But then randomly they gave him this test, and he was transferred to British intelligence in London. That's where he met my grandmother. But he had PTSD, next level. He just didn't talk. If you tried talking to him, he'd just tell you to shut up. He won a bunch of medals, never bragged about it once, not even hinted. I found out because his military records are online. He would say, 'No way in hell do you want to do that again.' But how many people now remember World War II? Not many. And the same perhaps applies to the threat of nuclear war.
turns.zh: 让我担心的一件事是，今天活着的人中，很少有人真正切身理解战争的恐怖，至少在美国是这样。我的意思是，显然在乌克兰和俄罗斯前线的人理解战争有多可怕。但西方有多少人理解呢？我的祖父参加过二战。他受到了严重的创伤。他在北非和意大利待了将近六年。他所有的朋友都在他面前被杀，他本来也会死，但碰巧他们随机进行了一些智商测试之类的，他得分很高。他不是军官；他是下士或中士，因为他没读完高中。他不得不辍学，因为他父亲去世了，他得工作养活兄弟姐妹。所以因为他没高中毕业，他没有资格成为军官。他基本上被归为炮灰。但后来他们随机给了他这个测试，他被调到了伦敦的英国情报机构。在那里他遇到了我的祖母。但他有创伤后应激障碍，非常严重。他就是不说话。如果你试图和他说话，他就叫你闭嘴。他赢得了一堆勋章，从未吹嘘过一次，甚至没有暗示过。我是因为他的军事记录在网上才知道的。他会说，‘你绝对不想再来一次。’但现在有多少人记得二战？不多了。同样的情况可能也适用于核战争的威胁。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, there are enough nuclear bombs pointed at the United States to make the radioactive rubble bounce many times. There are two major wars going on right now. You talked about the threat of AGI quite a bit, but now as we sit here with the intensity of conflict going on, do you worry about nuclear war?
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，有足够多的核弹瞄准美国，足以让放射性瓦砾多次弹跳。现在有两场大战正在进行。你谈了很多关于AGI的威胁，但现在我们坐在这里，冲突如此激烈，你担心核战争吗？

turns.en: I think we shouldn't discount the possibility of nuclear war. It is a civilizational threat. Right now, I could be wrong, but I think the current probability of nuclear war is quite low. But there are a lot of nukes pointed at us, and we have a lot of nukes pointed at other people. They're still there. Nobody has put their guns away. The missiles are still in the silos, and the leaders don't seem to be the ones with the nukes talking to each other.
turns.zh: 我认为我们不应该低估核战争的可能性。这是一种文明威胁。现在，我可能错了，但我认为当前核战争的概率相当低。但有很多核弹瞄准我们，我们也有很多核弹瞄准别人。它们还在那里。没有人放下武器。导弹还在发射井里，而拥有核武器的领导人似乎没有在互相交谈。

turns.en: There are wars which are tragic and difficult on a local basis, and then there are wars which are civilization-ending or have that potential. Obviously global thermonuclear warfare has high potential to end civilization, perhaps permanently, but certainly to severely wound and set back human progress to the Stone Age or something. I don't know, pretty bad. Probably scientists and engineers won't be super popular after that either. They'll be like, 'You got us into this mess.' So generally, we obviously want to prioritize civilizational risks over things that are painful and tragic on a local level but not civilizational.
turns.zh: 有些战争在局部层面是悲惨和艰难的，而有些战争则是文明终结或具有这种潜力。显然，全球热核战争极有可能终结文明，也许是永久性的，但肯定会严重伤害并阻碍人类进步，回到石器时代或类似情况。我不知道，非常糟糕。可能在那之后科学家和工程师也不会太受欢迎。他们会说，‘是你们把我们搞成这样的。’所以总的来说，我们显然应该优先考虑文明风险，而不是那些在局部层面痛苦悲惨但非文明性的问题。

turns.en: How do you hope the war in Ukraine comes to an end, and what's the path once again to minimizing human suffering there?
turns.zh: 你希望乌克兰战争如何结束？再次最小化那里人类苦难的路径是什么？

turns.en: Well, I think what is likely to happen, which is really pretty much the way it is, is that something very close to the current lines will be how a ceasefire or truce happens. But you know, you have a situation right now where whoever goes on the offensive will suffer casualties at several times the rate of whoever is on the defense, because you've got defense in depth: minefields, trenches, anti-tank defenses. Nobody has air superiority because anti-aircraft missiles are really far better than aircraft, and there are far more of them. So neither side has air superiority. Tanks are basically death traps, slow-moving and not immune to anti-tank weapons. So you really just have long-range artillery and infantry trenches. It's World War I all over again, with drones. Drones make the long-range artillery that much more accurate and better, so more efficient at murdering people on both sides. So you don't want to be trying to advance from either side because the probability of dying is incredibly high. In order to overcome defense in depth, trenches, and minefields, you really need significant local superiority in numbers, ideally combined with a fast attack with aircraft, a concentrated number of tanks, and a lot of people. That's the only way you're going to punch through a line and then not have reinforcements kick you right out again. I really recommend people read about World War I warfare in detail. It's rough. The sheer number of people that died was mind-boggling, and it's almost impossible to imagine the end of it that doesn't...
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为很可能发生的情况，实际上几乎就是现在这样，是停火或休战将沿着非常接近当前战线的地方发生。但你知道，现在的情况是，无论哪一方发动进攻，伤亡率都会是防守方的数倍，因为你有纵深防御：雷区、战壕、反坦克防御。没有人拥有空中优势，因为防空导弹远比飞机厉害，而且数量多得多。所以双方都没有空中优势。坦克基本上是死亡陷阱，移动缓慢，而且对反坦克武器不免疫。所以你实际上只有远程火炮和步兵战壕。这又是一战，只是多了无人机。无人机让远程火炮更加精确和有效，从而更高效地屠杀双方人员。所以任何一方都不想尝试推进，因为死亡概率极高。要克服纵深防御、战壕和雷区，你真的需要显著的局部数量优势，最好结合飞机快速攻击、集中数量的坦克和大量人员。这是你突破防线然后不被援军立刻赶回去的唯一方法。我真的建议人们详细阅读一战的历史。那很残酷。死亡人数之多令人难以置信，而且几乎无法想象一个不……的结局。

turns.en: It looks like almost exactly like the beginning in terms of who what land belongs to who and so on, but on the other side of a lot of human suffering, death, and destruction of infrastructure.
turns.zh: 看起来几乎和开始时一模一样，土地归属等等都没变，但另一边却是大量的人类苦难、死亡和基础设施的破坏。

turns.en: Yes. The reason I proposed some sort of truce or peace a year ago was because I predicted pretty much exactly what would happen, which is a lot of people dying for basically almost no changes in land. And this is the loss of the flower of Ukrainian and Russian youth. We should have some sympathy for the Russian boys as well as the Ukrainian boys, because the Russian boys didn't ask to be on the front line; they have to be. So there are a lot of sons not coming back to their parents. I think most of them don't really hate the other side. It's like the saying from World War I: young boys who don't know each other killing each other on behalf of old men that do know each other. What's the point of that?
turns.zh: 是的。我一年前提出某种休战或和平的原因，正是因为我几乎准确预测了会发生什么——大量的人死去，而领土基本没有变化。这是乌克兰和俄罗斯青年精英的损失。我们应该对俄罗斯男孩和乌克兰男孩都抱有同情，因为俄罗斯男孩并非自愿上前线，他们不得不去。很多儿子再也回不到父母身边。我认为他们大多数并不真正仇恨对方。就像一战时的那句话：互不相识的年轻男孩，替彼此认识的老男人互相残杀。这有什么意义呢？

turns.en: Volodymyr Zelenskyy has said in the past he's not interested in talking to Putin directly. Do you think he should?
turns.zh: 泽连斯基过去说过他对直接与普京对话不感兴趣。你认为他应该谈吗？

turns.en: Yeah, sit down man-to-man, leader to leader, and negotiate peace. Look, I think I would just recommend: do not send the flower of Ukrainian youth to die in trenches. Whether he talks to Putin or not, just don't do that. Whoever goes on the offensive will lose massive numbers of people, and history will not look kindly upon them.
turns.zh: 是的，面对面坐下来，领袖对领袖，谈判和平。听着，我只建议：不要把乌克兰的青年精英送去战壕里送死。无论他是否与普京对话，都不要这样做。任何发动进攻的一方都将损失大量人员，历史不会善待他们。

transcript.secZh: 乌克兰战争与和平谈判

turns.en: You've spoken honestly about the possibility of war between the US and China in the long term if no diplomatic solution is found, for example on the question of Taiwan and the One China policy. How do we avoid the trajectory where these two superpowers clash?
turns.zh: 你曾坦诚地谈到，如果找不到外交解决方案，比如在台湾问题和一中政策上，美中长期来看可能发生战争。我们如何避免这两个超级大国冲突的轨迹？

turns.en: Well, it's worth reading that book on the difficult-to-pronounce Thucydides Trap. I love war history. I like Sun Tzu and Art of War backwards. There's hardly a battle I haven't read about, trying to figure out what really was the cause of victory in any particular case, as opposed to what one side or another claimed. Both the victory and what sparked the war. The whole thing. So Athens and Sparta is a classic case. The thing about the Greeks is they really wrote down a lot of stuff; they loved writing. There are lots of interesting things that happened in many parts of the world, but people just didn't write down, so we don't know what happened. Or they didn't write in detail; they'd just say, 'We had a battle and we won.' Like, can you add a bit more? The Greeks really wrote a lot; they were very articulate and just loved writing. We have a bunch of that writing preserved, so we know what led up to the Peloponnesian War between the Spartan and Athenian alliances. We know that they saw it coming. The Spartans didn't write; they weren't very verbose by nature. But the Athenians and other Greeks wrote a lot. They were like, 'Sparta was really the leader of Greece, but Athens grew stronger with each passing year.' Everyone thought it was inevitable that there would be a clash between Athens and Sparta. 'How do we avoid that?' And they couldn't. They actually saw it coming and still could not avoid it. So at some point, if one group, civilization, or country exceeds another... The United States has been the biggest kid on the block since around 1890 from an economic standpoint. The US has been the most powerful economic engine in the world longer than anyone's been alive. The foundation of war is economics. Now we have a situation with China where the economy is likely to be two, perhaps three times larger than that of the US. So imagine you're the biggest kid on the block for as long as anyone can remember, and suddenly a kid comes along who's twice your size. We see it coming. How is it possible to stop?
turns.zh: 嗯，值得一读那本名字难念的《修昔底德陷阱》。我热爱战争史，倒背《孙子兵法》和《战争艺术》。几乎没有我没读过的战役，我试图弄清楚每场胜利的真正原因，而不是某一方声称的原因。无论是胜利还是战争的起因，整个事情。雅典和斯巴达就是经典案例。希腊人的特点是他们记录了很多东西，他们热爱写作。世界上很多地方发生了很多有趣的事，但人们没有记录下来，所以我们不知道发生了什么。或者他们记录得不详细，只会说‘我们打了一仗，赢了’。能多说点吗？希腊人真的写了很多，他们口才很好，就是热爱写作。我们保存了其中一些文字，所以知道斯巴达和雅典联盟之间伯罗奔尼撒战争的起因。我们知道他们预见到了。斯巴达人不怎么写，他们天性不善言辞。但雅典人和其他希腊人写了很多。他们说：‘斯巴达曾是希腊的领袖，但雅典一年比一年强大。’大家都认为雅典和斯巴达之间的冲突不可避免。‘我们如何避免？’他们做不到。他们实际上预见到了，但仍然无法避免。所以到了某个点，如果一个群体、文明或国家超过了另一个……美国自1890年左右以来一直是经济上最大的玩家。美国成为世界上最强大的经济引擎的时间比任何在世的人都要长。战争的基础是经济。现在中国的情况是，其经济规模可能是美国的两到三倍。想象一下，你是有史以来最大的玩家，突然来了一个比你大一倍的孩子。我们预见到了。怎么可能阻止呢？

turns.en: Is there some intermixing of cultures, understanding? There does seem to be a giant cultural gap in understanding of each other. You're an interesting case study because you are an American, you've done a lot of manufacturing here in the US, but you also work with China. I've spent a lot of time in China and met with the leadership many times. Maybe a good question to ask is: what are some things about China that people don't understand, positive just in the culture? What interesting things have you learned about the Chinese?
turns.zh: 有没有一些文化交融和理解？双方似乎存在巨大的文化理解鸿沟。你是一个有趣的案例，因为你是美国人，在美国做了很多制造业，但你也与中国合作。我在中国待过很长时间，多次见过中国领导人。也许一个好的问题是：关于中国，有哪些人们不了解的、文化上积极的东西？你学到了哪些关于中国人的有趣事情？

turns.en: Well, the sheer number of really smart, hardworking people in China is incredible. There are far more of them there than there are here, in my opinion. And they've got a lot of energy. The architecture in China in recent years is far more impressive than in the US. The train stations, the buildings, the high-speed rail, everything is really far more impressive than what we have in the US. I recommend somebody just go to Shanghai and Beijing, look at the buildings, and take the train from Beijing to Xi'an where you have the Terracotta Warriors. China has incredible history, a very long history. I think arguably, in terms of the use of language from a written standpoint, it's one of the oldest, perhaps the oldest written language. And Chinese people did write things down. Now, China historically has always been, with rare exception, internally focused. They have not been inquisitive. They fought each other; there have been many civil wars. In the Three Kingdoms War, I believe they lost about 70% of their population. So they've had brutal internal wars that make the US Civil War look small by comparison. I think it's important to appreciate that China is not monolithic. We sort of think of China as a single entity of one mind, and this is definitely not the case. From what I've seen, and I think most people who understand China would agree, people in China think about China ten times more than they think about anything outside of China. So 90% of their consideration is internal.
turns.zh: 嗯，中国真正聪明勤奋的人数量惊人。在我看来，那里比这里多得多。而且他们精力充沛。近年来中国的建筑比美国令人印象深刻得多。火车站、高楼、高铁，一切都比我们美国的好得多。我建议人们去上海和北京看看那些建筑，然后坐火车从北京到西安看兵马俑。中国有令人难以置信的历史，非常悠久。我认为，从文字使用的角度来看，它是最古老的语言之一，也许是最古老的。而且中国人确实记录了很多东西。现在，历史上中国除了极少数例外，一直专注于内部。他们不好奇。他们互相争斗，内战很多。在三国战争期间，我相信他们损失了大约70%的人口。所以他们有过残酷的内战，相比之下美国内战都显得规模小了。我认为重要的是要认识到中国不是铁板一块。我们有点把中国看作一个单一的整体，有统一的思想，但这绝对不是事实。根据我的观察，而且我认为大多数了解中国的人都会同意，中国人思考中国内部事务的时间是思考外部事务的十倍。所以他们的考虑90%是内部的。

turns.en: Isn't that a really positive thing when you're talking about collaboration in a future peace between superpowers? When you're inward-facing, focusing on improving yourself versus focusing on improving others through military might.
turns.zh: 当谈到超级大国之间未来和平合作时，这难道不是一件非常积极的事情吗？当你向内看，专注于提升自己，而不是通过军事力量去‘提升’别人。

turns.en: The good news: the history of China...
turns.zh: 好消息是：中国的历史……

transcript.secZh: 中美关系与修昔底德陷阱

transcript.sec: China and US as non-inquisitive powers

turns.en: Suggests that China is not inquisitive, meaning they're not going to go out and invade a whole bunch of countries. Now they do feel very strongly, you know. So that's good, I mean because a lot of very powerful countries have been inquisitive. The US is one of the also one of the rare cases that has not been inquisitive. Like after World War II, the US could have basically taken over the world and any country. Like we got nukes, nobody else got nukes. We don't even have to lose soldiers. Which country do you want? And the United States could have taken over everything. And it didn't. The United States actually helped rebuild countries, so it helped rebuild Europe, you know, helped rebuild Japan. This is very unusual behavior, almost unprecedented. You know, the US did conspicuous acts of kindness like the Berlin airlift. And I think you know, there it's always like, well America's done bad things. Well of course America's done bad things, but one needs to look at the whole track record. And generally, one sort of test would be how do you treat your prisoners of war. Or let's say, no offense to the Russians, but let's say you're in Germany in 1945, you got the Russian army coming one side, you got the French, British and American armies coming the other side. Who would you like to surrender to? No country is perfectly moral, but I recommend being a POW with the Americans. That would be my choice very strongly. And in fact, Von Braun, smart guy, was like, we've got to be captured by the Americans. And in fact, the SS was under orders to execute Von Braun and all of the German rocket engineers. And they narrowly escaped. They said they were going out for a walk in the woods. They left in the middle of winter with no coats, and they ran with no food, no coats, no water, and just ran like hell, and ran west. And by some luck, I think his brother found a bicycle or something, and then just cycled west as fast as he could and found a US patrol. So anyway, that's one way you can tell morality: who do you want to be a POW? It's not fun anywhere, but some places are much worse than others. So anyway, America has been, while far from perfect, generally a benevolent force. And we should always be self-critical and try to be better. But anyone with half a brain knows that. So I think in this way, China and the United States are similar. Neither country has been inquisitive in a significant way. So that's like a shared principle, I guess.
turns.zh: 这表明中国并不具有侵略性，意思是他们不会去入侵一大堆国家。不过他们确实有很强的立场，这很好。因为很多非常强大的国家都具有侵略性。美国也是少数不具有侵略性的国家之一。比如二战后，美国本可以基本上接管全世界。我们有核武器，其他国家没有。我们甚至不用损失士兵。你想要哪个国家？美国本可以接管一切，但它没有。美国实际上帮助重建了其他国家，比如帮助重建了欧洲，帮助重建了日本。这是非常不寻常的行为，几乎是史无前例的。美国做了像柏林空运这样引人注目的善举。而且我想，人们总是说，美国做过坏事。当然美国做过坏事，但需要看整体记录。一般来说，一个检验标准是你如何对待战俘。或者说，无意冒犯俄罗斯人，但假设你在1945年的德国，一边是苏联军队，另一边是法、英、美军队，你愿意向谁投降？没有哪个国家是完美道德的，但我建议成为美国的战俘。那会是我的强烈选择。事实上，冯·布劳恩，聪明人，就说我们必须被美国人俘虏。实际上，党卫军曾接到命令处决冯·布劳恩和所有德国火箭工程师。他们侥幸逃脱。他们说要去树林里散步，在隆冬时节没有穿外套就离开了，然后没有食物、没有外套、没有水，拼命向西跑。幸运的是，他兄弟找到了一辆自行车之类的，然后他尽可能快地骑向西，找到了一支美军巡逻队。所以，这是判断道德的一种方式：你想成为哪国的战俘？哪里都不好玩，但有些地方比别的地方糟糕得多。所以，美国虽然远非完美，但总体上是一股仁慈的力量。我们应该总是自我批评，努力变得更好。但任何有半个脑子的人都知道这一点。所以我认为在这方面，中国和美国是相似的。两个国家都没有显著的侵略性。所以这大概是一个共同原则。

transcript.secZh: 中美作为非探究性大国

turns.en: Now China does feel very strongly about Taiwan. They've been very clear about that for a long time. From their standpoint, it would be like one of the states is not there, like Hawaii or something like that, but more significant than Hawaii. Hawaii is pretty significant for us. So they view it as really a fundamental part of China. The island of Formosa, not Taiwan, that is not part of China but should be. And the only reason it hasn't been is because of the US Pacific Fleet. And as their economic power grows and as their military power grows, the thing that they are clearly saying is their interest will clearly be materialized. Yes, China has been very clear that they will incorporate Taiwan peacefully or militarily, but that they will incorporate it. From their standpoint, it's 100% likely.
turns.zh: 现在中国对台湾问题立场非常坚定。他们长期以来一直非常明确。从他们的角度看，这就像某个州不在了，比如夏威夷之类的，但比夏威夷更重要。夏威夷对我们来说也很重要。所以他们将其视为中国不可分割的一部分。福尔摩沙岛，不是台湾，它不属于中国但应该属于。而它之所以还没有，是因为美国太平洋舰队。随着经济实力和军事实力的增长，他们明确表示的利益将会实现。是的，中国已经非常明确，他们将和平或军事地统一台湾，但一定会统一。从他们的角度看，这是100%确定的。

transcript.sec: Conspicuous acts of kindness as policy

turns.en: Something you said about conspicuous acts of kindness as a geopolitical policy, it almost seems naive, but I'd venture to say that this is probably the path forward, how you avoid most wars. Just as you say, it sounds naive but it's kind of brilliant if you believe in the goodness underlying most of human nature. It just seems like conspicuous acts of kindness can reverberate through the populace of the countries involved, yeah, and de-escalate.
turns.zh: 你提到的引人注目的善举作为一种地缘政治策略，听起来几乎有些天真，但我敢说这可能是前进的道路，是避免大多数战争的方法。就像你说的，听起来天真但很聪明，如果你相信人性本善。似乎引人注目的善举可以在相关国家民众中产生共鸣，并缓和局势。

turns.en: Absolutely. So after World War I, they made a big mistake. They basically tried to lump all the blame on Germany, and settled Germany with impossible reparations. And there was a fair bit of blame to go around for World War I, but they tried to put it all on Germany. And that laid the seeds for World War II. A lot of people, not just Hitler, felt wronged and wanted vengeance, and they got it. People don't forget. You kill somebody's father, mother, son, daughter, they're not going to forget it. They will want vengeance. So after World War II, they were like, the Treaty of Versailles was a huge mistake in World War I. So this time, instead of crushing the losers, we're actually going to help them with the Marshall Plan, and we're going to help rebuild Germany, Austria, Italy, and whatnot. So that was the right move.
turns.zh: 完全正确。所以一战后，他们犯了一个大错误。他们基本上把所有责任都推给德国，并让德国承担不可能实现的赔款。一战的责任各方都有份，但他们试图全部归咎于德国。这为二战埋下了种子。很多人，不仅仅是希特勒，感到被冤枉并想要复仇，他们得到了。人们不会忘记。你杀了某人的父亲、母亲、儿子、女儿，他们不会忘记。他们会想要复仇。所以二战后，他们认为《凡尔赛条约》是一战中的巨大错误。所以这次，他们没有打压战败国，而是通过马歇尔计划帮助他们，重建德国、奥地利、意大利等等。所以那是正确的做法。

turns.en: There's a profound truth to the conspicuous acts of kindness being an antidote to this. Something must stop the cycle of reciprocal violence. Something must stop it or it will never stop. Eye for an eye, tooth for a tooth, limb for limb, life for a life, forever and ever.
turns.zh: 引人注目的善举作为解药，这其中有一个深刻的真理。必须有人阻止相互暴力的循环。必须有人阻止它，否则它永远不会停止。以眼还眼，以牙还牙，以肢体还肢体，以命还命，永无止境。

transcript.sec: Grok AI and philosophy of curiosity

turns.en: To escape briefly the darkness, there was some incredible engineering work. xAI just released Grok AI assistant that I've gotten a chance to play with. It's amazing on many levels. First of all, it's amazing that a relatively small team in a relatively short amount of time was able to develop this close to state-of-the-art system. Another incredible thing is there's a regular mode and a fun mode. I guess I'm to blame for that one. I wish first of all everything in life had a fun mode. There's something compelling beyond just fun about the fun mode. Interacting with a large language model, I'm not sure exactly what it is because I only had a little bit of time to play with it, but it just makes it more interesting, more vibrant to interact with the system.
turns.zh: 为了暂时逃离黑暗，有一些令人难以置信的工程工作。xAI 刚刚发布了 Grok AI 助手，我有机会试用了一下。它在很多层面上都令人惊叹。首先，一个相对较小的团队在相对较短的时间内开发出接近最先进的系统，这很了不起。另一个令人难以置信的事情是，它有普通模式和趣味模式。我想这得怪我。我希望生活中所有事情都有趣味模式。趣味模式除了有趣之外，还有一些引人入胜的东西。与大型语言模型互动，我不确定具体是什么，因为我只玩了一小会儿，但它让与系统的互动更有趣、更有活力。

turns.en: Absolutely. Our AI Grok is modeled after the Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, which is one of my favorite books. It's a book on philosophy disguised as a book on humor. And I would say that forms the basis of my philosophy, which is that we don't know the meaning of life, but the more we can expand the scope and scale of consciousness, digital and biological, the more we are able to understand what questions to ask about the universe. So I have a philosophy of curiosity. There is generally a feeling like this AI system has an outward looking, like the way you are sitting with a good friend looking up at the stars, asking profound questions.
turns.zh: 完全正确。我们的 AI Grok 是以《银河系漫游指南》为原型的，那是我最喜欢的书之一。它是一本伪装成幽默书的哲学书。我想这构成了我哲学的基础，那就是我们不知道生命的意义，但我们越能扩展意识的范围和规模，无论是数字的还是生物的，我们就越能理解该问宇宙什么问题。所以我有一个好奇的哲学。通常感觉这个 AI 系统有一种向外看的特质，就像你和好朋友坐在一起仰望星空，提出深刻的问题。

transcript.sec: Pursuing Truth and Reliability in AI

turns.en: The universe wondering what it's all about. The curiosity you talk about, there's a sense, no matter how I'm on a day, the question I ask it, there's a sense of cosmic grandeur to the whole thing.
turns.zh: 宇宙在好奇它自己是怎么回事。你谈到的那种好奇心，无论我处于何种状态，我问出的问题都带有一种宇宙的宏大感。

turns.en: Well, we are actually working hard to have engineering, math, and physics answers that you can count on. For the other sort of AIs out there, these so-called large language models, I've not found the engineering to be reliable. And the hallucination, it unfortunately hallucinates most when you least want it to hallucinate. So when you ask important, difficult questions, that's when it tends to be confidently wrong. So we're really trying hard to say, okay, how do we be as grounded as possible so you can count on the results? Trace things back to physics first principles, mathematical logic. So underlying the humor is an aspiration to adhere to the truth of the universe as closely as possible. That's really tricky. It is tricky. So that's why there's always going to be some amount of error, but do we want to aspire to be as truthful as possible about the answers with acknowledged error? So you don't want to be confidently wrong. You're not going to be right every time, but you want to minimize how often you're confidently wrong. And then, like I said, once you can count on the logic as not violating physics, then you can start to build on that to create inventions, like invent new technologies. But if you cannot count on the foundational physics being correct, obviously the inventions are simply wishful thinking, imagination land, magic basically.
turns.zh: 我们实际上在努力提供可靠的工程、数学和物理答案。对于其他那些所谓的大语言模型，我发现它们的工程并不可靠。而且幻觉问题，不幸的是，它在你最不希望它出现的时候出现得最多。所以当你问重要、困难的问题时，它往往自信地给出错误答案。所以我们真的在努力思考：如何尽可能脚踏实地，让你能信赖结果？追溯到物理第一原理、数学逻辑。所以幽默背后是一种尽可能贴近宇宙真理的渴望。这非常棘手。确实棘手。所以总会有一定误差，但我们是否希望尽可能真实地回答，并承认误差？你不想自信地犯错。你不可能每次都正确，但你要尽量减少自信犯错的次数。而且，就像我说的，一旦你能信赖逻辑不违反物理，你就可以在此基础上创造发明，比如新技术。但如果你不能信赖基础物理是正确的，那么发明显然只是空想、想象世界、基本上是魔法。

turns.en: As you said, I think one of the big goals of xAI is to understand the universe. Yes, that's a simple three-word mission. If you look out far into the future, do you think on this level of physics, the very edge of what we understand about physics, do you think it will make discoveries? Sort of the sexiest discovery of them is, as we know now, unifying general relativity and quantum mechanics, so coming up with a Theory of Everything. Do you think it could push towards that direction, almost like theoretical physics discoveries?
turns.zh: 正如你所说，我认为 xAI 的一大目标就是理解宇宙。是的，就是那个简单的三个字使命。如果展望遥远的未来，你认为在这个物理层面，在我们对物理理解的边缘，它会做出发现吗？最性感的发现之一，正如我们现在所知，是统一广义相对论和量子力学，提出一个万有理论。你认为它会朝那个方向推进吗，几乎像理论物理的发现？

turns.en: If an AI cannot figure out new physics, it's clearly not equal to humans, let alone surpass humans, because humans have figured out new physics. Physics is just understanding the deep insight into how reality works. And then engineering is inventing things that have never existed. Now, the range of possibilities for engineering is far greater than for physics, because once you figure out the rules of the universe, that's it—you've discovered things that already existed. But from that, you can then build technologies that are really almost limitless in variety. It's like once you understand the rules of the game properly, and we do—current physics, at least at a local level, understand how physics works very well. Our ability to predict things is incredibly good. Quantum mechanics, the degree to which quantum mechanics can predict outcomes, is incredible. That was my hardest class in college, by the way—my senior quantum mechanics class was harder than all of my other classes put together. To get an AI system, a large language model, to reliably be as reliable as quantum mechanics and physics is very difficult. You have to test any conclusions against the ground truth of reality. Reality is the ultimate judge. Physics is a law; everything else is a recommendation. I've seen plenty of people break the laws made by man, but none break the laws made by physics. It's a good test: if this LLM understands and matches physics, then you can more reliably trust whatever it thinks about the current state of politics in some sense. And it's also not the case currently that even its internal logic is consistent. Especially with the approach of just predicting a token, predict token, predict token—it's like a vector sum, you're summing up a bunch of vectors, but you can get drift. So a little bit of error, a little bit of error adds up, and by the time you are many tokens down the path, it doesn't make any sense. So it has to be somehow self-aware about the drift, and then look at the thing as a whole and say, does it have coherence as a whole? When authors write books, they will write the book and then go and revise it, taking into account the end and the beginning and the middle, and rewrite it to achieve coherence so that it doesn't end up in a nonsensical place. Maybe the process of revising is what reasoning is, and that's the process of revising is how you get closer and closer to truth. At least I approach that way: you just say a bunch first, and then you get it better. You start, you create a draft, and then you iterate on that draft until it has coherence, until it adds up basically.
turns.zh: 如果一个人工智能不能发现新的物理学，那它显然不如人类，更不用说超越人类了，因为人类已经发现了新的物理学。物理学就是理解现实如何运作的深刻洞察。而工程是发明从未存在过的东西。工程的可能性范围远大于物理学，因为一旦你弄清了宇宙的规则，那就是了——你发现了已经存在的东西。但在此基础上，你可以构建几乎无限多样的技术。就像一旦你正确理解了游戏规则，而我们确实理解——当前的物理学，至少在局部层面，对物理如何运作理解得很好。我们预测事物的能力非常出色。量子力学，量子力学预测结果的程度令人难以置信。顺便说一句，那是我大学里最难的一门课——我的高年级量子力学课比所有其他课加起来还难。让一个人工智能系统，一个大语言模型，可靠得像量子力学和物理学一样非常困难。你必须用现实的真实情况来检验任何结论。现实是最终的裁判。物理学是法律，其他一切都是建议。我见过很多人违反人造的法律，但没有人违反物理定律。这是一个很好的测试：如果这个大语言模型理解并匹配物理学，那么你就可以更可靠地信任它对当前政治状况的看法。而且目前的情况是，甚至它的内部逻辑也不一致。尤其是那种仅仅预测下一个词、预测下一个词、预测下一个词的方法——就像向量求和，你把一堆向量加起来，但会出现漂移。所以一点点误差，一点点误差累积起来，当你走了很多词之后，它就毫无意义了。所以它必须以某种方式意识到漂移，然后从整体上看待事物，并说它整体上是否具有连贯性。当作者写书时，他们会写书，然后去修改，考虑结尾、开头和中间，并重写以实现连贯性，这样就不会最终陷入无意义的地方。也许修改的过程就是推理，而修改的过程就是如何越来越接近真理。至少我是这样做的：你先说一堆，然后让它变得更好。你开始，你创建一个草稿，然后你迭代那个草稿，直到它具有连贯性，直到它基本上说得通。

turns.en: Another question about Theory of Everything, but for intelligence. Do you think there exists, as you're exploring this with xAI creating this intelligence system, do you think there is a theory of intelligence where you get to understand what is the 'I' in AGI and what is the 'I' in human intelligence? There's no 'I' in Team America—oh wait, there is. That's going to be stuck in my head now. There's no 'me' in whatever. In quantum mechanics, oh wait. I mean, is that part of the process of discovering understanding the universe is understanding intelligence?
turns.zh: 另一个关于万有理论的问题，但针对智能。你认为，当你用 xAI 创建这个智能系统时，是否存在一个智能理论，让你理解 AGI 中的“I”是什么，人类智能中的“I”是什么？Team America 里没有“I”——哦等等，有的。这句话要卡在我脑子里了。无论什么里都没有“我”。在量子力学里，哦等等。我的意思是，发现理解宇宙的过程就是理解智能吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I think we need to understand intelligence, understand consciousness. I mean, there are some fundamental questions like what is thought, what is emotion. Is it really just one atom bumping into another atom? It feels like something more than that. So I think we're probably missing some really big things, something that'll be obvious in retrospect. Like there's a giant—you put the whole consciousness, emotion—well, some would call it like a soul, you know, religion a soul. Like you feel like you're you, right? You don't feel like you're just a collection of atoms. But on what dimension does thought exist? What dimension do emotions exist? We feel them very strongly. I suspect there's more to it than atoms bumping into atoms, and maybe AI can pave the path to the discovery of whatever that thing is. What is consciousness? Like when you put the atoms in a particular shape, why are they able to form thoughts and take actions and feelings? Even if it is an illusion.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为我们需要理解智能，理解意识。我的意思是，有一些基本问题，比如什么是思想，什么是情感。真的只是一个原子撞到另一个原子吗？感觉远不止如此。所以我认为我们可能遗漏了一些非常重要的东西，一些事后看来会很明显的东西。就像有一个巨大的——你把整个意识、情感——嗯，有些人会称之为灵魂，你知道，宗教里的灵魂。就像你感觉你是你，对吧？你不觉得自己只是一堆原子。但思想存在于什么维度？情感存在于什么维度？我们强烈地感受到它们。我怀疑这不仅仅是原子撞原子，也许人工智能可以为发现那个东西铺平道路。意识是什么？当你把原子排列成特定形状时，为什么它们能够形成思想、采取行动和产生感觉？即使那是一种幻觉。

turns.en: Why is this illusion so compelling? How does the solution exist? On what plane does this solution exist? Sometimes I wonder: either everything is conscious or nothing is conscious. I like the former—everything conscious just seems more fun.
turns.zh: 为什么这个幻觉如此引人入胜？解决方案如何存在？它存在于哪个层面？有时我想：要么万物皆有意识，要么万物皆无意识。我喜欢前者——万物皆有意识似乎更有趣。

turns.en: It does seem more fun. But we're composed of atoms, and those atoms are composed of quarks and leptons, which have been around since the beginning of the universe—what seems to be the beginning of the universe.
turns.zh: 确实更有趣。但我们由原子组成，原子又由夸克和轻子组成，这些粒子自宇宙诞生之初就存在了——至少是宇宙看似诞生之时。

turns.en: The first time we talked, you said something surreal: you asked what question you would ask an AGI system once you create it, and you said, 'What's outside the simulation?' Good question. But it seems like with Grok, you've started to literally build a system whose goal is to be able to ask and answer such questions.
turns.zh: 我们第一次谈话时，你说了一些超现实的话：我问你，一旦创造出AGI系统，你会问它什么问题，你说'模拟之外是什么？'好问题。但似乎通过Grok，你真的开始构建一个系统，其目标就是能够提出并回答这类问题。

turns.en: Where are the aliens? That's one of the Fermi Paradox questions. A lot of people have asked me if I've seen any evidence of aliens, and I haven't. That's kind of concerning. I would probably prefer to have seen some archaeological evidence of aliens. To the best of my knowledge, there is no evidence of aliens out there. They're very subtle. We might just be the only consciousness, at least in the galaxy.
turns.zh: 外星人在哪里？这是费米悖论的问题之一。很多人问我是否见过外星人的证据，我没有。这有点令人担忧。我宁愿看到一些外星人的考古证据。据我所知，没有外星人的证据。他们非常隐蔽。我们可能是唯一的意识，至少在银河系里。

turns.en: If you look at Earth's history, the Earth is about 4.5 billion years old. Civilization, measured from the first writing, is only about 5,000 years old. That's one millionth of Earth's existence. Civilization has been around for a flash in the pan. Why did it take so long? For the vast majority of time, there was no life, then archaic bacteria, then mitochondria, multicellular life, plants and animals, life moving from oceans to land, mammals, higher brain functions. The Sun is expanding slowly; it will overheat the Earth, boil the oceans, and Earth will become like Venus. If we don't become multiplanetary and go beyond our solar system, annihilation of all life on Earth is a certainty. It could be as little as half a billion years—a long time by human standards, but only 10% longer than Earth has existed. If life had taken 10% longer to evolve, it wouldn't exist at all. We have a deadline.
turns.zh: 看看地球的历史，地球大约45亿岁。文明，从最早的文字算起，只有大约5000年。那是地球存在时间的百万分之一。文明只是昙花一现。为什么花了这么久？绝大部分时间没有生命，然后是原始细菌，线粒体，多细胞生命，植物和动物，生命从海洋到陆地，哺乳动物，高级大脑功能。太阳在缓慢膨胀；它会使地球过热，煮沸海洋，地球会变得像金星。如果我们不成为多行星物种并走出太阳系，地球上所有生命的毁灭是确定的。可能只有5亿年——按人类标准是很长时间，但只比地球存在时间长10%。如果生命进化多花了10%，它根本就不会存在。我们有截止日期。

turns.en: Better hurry. But as you said, intelligent life on Earth developed a lot of cool stuff very quickly. Becoming multiplanetary seems almost inevitable unless we destroy ourselves. I suspect that if we explore other star systems, we might find a whole bunch of long-dead one-planet civilizations. They never made it past their home planet. That's sad, but also fascinating.
turns.zh: 最好快点。但如你所说，地球上的智能生命很快发展了很多酷东西。成为多行星物种几乎是不可避免的，除非我们自我毁灭。我怀疑如果我们探索其他恒星系统，可能会发现一大堆早已灭亡的单行星文明。他们从未离开过母星。这很可悲，但也令人着迷。

turns.en: That's so sad. Also fascinating. There are various explanations for the Fermi Paradox. One is the great filters—civilizations don't pass through them. One filter is: do you become a multi-planet civilization? If not, it's only a matter of time before something happens on your planet, natural or man-made, that causes extinction. Like the dinosaurs—where are they now? They didn't have spaceships.
turns.zh: 太可悲了。但也令人着迷。费米悖论有多种解释。一个是'大过滤器'——文明无法通过。一个过滤器是：你是否成为多行星文明？如果不，你的星球上迟早会发生自然或人为的灾难导致灭绝。就像恐龙——它们现在在哪？它们没有宇宙飞船。

turns.en: I think the more likely thing is that the aliens found us and are protecting us, letting us be. Like the uncontacted tribes in the Amazon—we protect them. That would be a nice explanation. Or you could have what Andre Karthi said: it's like the ants in the Amazon asking 'Where is everybody?' Well, they do run into a lot of other ants. There are ant wars. Maybe I'm dismissing the diversity of evidence. You should listen to Werner Herzog talking about the jungle—it's hilarious.
turns.zh: 我认为更可能的是外星人发现了我们，并在保护我们，让我们自由发展。就像亚马逊的未接触部落——我们保护他们。那会是一个很好的解释。或者像安德烈·卡蒂说的：就像亚马逊的蚂蚁问'大家都在哪里？'嗯，它们确实会遇到很多其他蚂蚁。还有蚂蚁战争。也许我忽略了证据的多样性。你应该听听沃纳·赫尔佐格谈论丛林——非常搞笑。

turns.en: I haven't heard that. Werner Herzog is a way—you should play it as an interlude. It's on YouTube, it's awesome. I love him so much. He was the director of 'Happy People: Life in the Taiga'? Also the bear documentary and the one about penguins—the psychoanalysis of penguins. Penguins heading for mountains 70 miles away, and the penguin is doomed. He had a cynical take. But he could be a brave explorer, and great stories will be told about him among the penguin population for centuries.
turns.zh: 我没听过。沃纳·赫尔佐格是个奇人——你应该放一段作为插曲。在YouTube上，很棒。我非常喜欢他。他导演了《快乐的人们：泰加林生活》？还有熊的纪录片和关于企鹅的——企鹅的精神分析。企鹅走向70英里外的山脉，那只企鹅注定要完。他持愤世嫉俗的观点。但他可能是个勇敢的探险家，企鹅群中会流传关于他的伟大故事几个世纪。

turns.en: What are we talking about? Okay, aliens. I think the smart move is that this is the first time in Earth's history that it's been possible for life to extend beyond Earth. That window is open now. It may be open for a long time or a short time, and it may be open now and never again. So the smart move is to make life multiplanetary while it's possible. We don't want to be one of those lame one-planet civilizations that just dies out. Those are lame. A self-respecting civilization wouldn't be one-planet. There won't be a Wikipedia entry for one of those.
turns.zh: 我们在说什么？好吧，外星人。我认为明智之举是：这是地球历史上第一次有可能让生命延伸到地球之外。那扇窗现在打开了。它可能开很久，也可能开很短，而且可能现在开了就再也不会开。所以明智之举是在可能的时候让生命成为多行星物种。我们不想成为那些可悲的单行星文明，就那么灭绝了。那些太可悲了。一个有自尊的文明不会只待在一个星球上。不会有维基百科条目记录它们。

turns.en: Does SpaceX have an official policy for when we meet aliens?
turns.zh: SpaceX有关于我们遇到外星人时的官方政策吗？

turns.en: No. That seems irresponsible. Look, if I see the slightest indication of aliens, I will immediately post on the next platform. It could be the most liked, reposted post of all time. We have more satellites up there than everyone else combined, so we would know if we had to maneuver around something. But we don't.
turns.zh: 没有。这似乎不负责任。听着，如果我看到任何外星人的迹象，我会立即在下一个平台上发帖。那可能是史上点赞和转发最多的帖子。我们在太空的卫星比所有其他国家的总和还多，所以如果我们需要躲避什么东西，我们会知道。但我们没有。

turns.en: You've said you're with Einstein, believing in the God of Spinoza. That's a view that God is like the universe and reveals himself through the laws of physics.
turns.zh: 你说过你赞同爱因斯坦，相信斯宾诺莎的上帝。那是一种观点：上帝就像宇宙，通过物理定律显现自己。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Einstein said through the lawful harmony of the world... I would agree that the God of the simulator, or whatever the Supreme Being or beings, themselves through the physics, the creators of this existence, and it's incumbent upon us to try to understand more about this wondrous creation. Like who created this thing, who's running this thing? Embodying it into a singular question with a sexy word on top of it is like focusing the mind to understand it. It does seem like there's a purpose, that there's an underlying master plan of some kind. It seems like there may not be a master plan in the sense... So this might be an interesting answer to the question of determinism versus free will: if we are in a simulation, the reason the higher beings would run a simulation is to see what happens. They don't know what happens, otherwise they wouldn't run the simulation.
turns.zh: 爱因斯坦说过，通过世界的和谐法则……我同意，模拟器的上帝，或者任何至高存在，通过物理法则，这个存在的创造者，我们有责任去理解这个奇妙的造物。比如谁创造了它，谁在运行它？把它浓缩成一个带有性感词汇的单一问题，就像集中思想去理解它。确实看起来有某种目的，有某种潜在的宏大计划。但似乎可能并没有一个宏大计划……所以这可能是对决定论与自由意志问题的一个有趣回答：如果我们身处模拟中，高等存在运行模拟的原因是为了看看会发生什么。他们不知道会发生什么，否则就不会运行模拟了。

turns.en: When humans create a simulation, at SpaceX and Tesla we create simulations all the time, especially for the rocket. You have to run a lot of simulations to understand what's going to happen because you can't really test the rocket until it goes to space, and you want it to work. So you have to simulate subsonic, transonic, supersonic, hypersonic ascent, and then coming back, super high heating, and orbital dynamics. All this has to be simulated. You don't get very many kicks at the can. We run the simulations to see what happens, not because we know what happens. So if whoever created this existence is running it, they're running it because they don't know what's going to happen, not because they do.
turns.zh: 当人类创建模拟时，在SpaceX和特斯拉，我们一直在创建模拟，尤其是针对火箭。你必须运行大量模拟来了解会发生什么，因为你无法真正测试火箭直到它进入太空，而你希望它能正常工作。所以你必须模拟亚音速、跨音速、超音速、高超音速上升，然后返回，极高的加热，以及轨道动力学。所有这些都必须模拟。你没有太多机会。我们运行模拟是为了看看会发生什么，而不是因为我们知道会发生什么。所以如果创造这个存在的那个家伙在运行它，他们运行它是因为他们不知道会发生什么，而不是因为他们知道。

turns.en: Maybe we both play Diablo. Maybe Diablo was created to see if a druid, your character, could defeat Uber Lilith at the end. They didn't know. Well, the funny thing is, her title is Hatred Incarnate. Right now, I guess you can ask the Diablo team, but it's almost impossible to defeat Hatred in the Eternal realm. You've streamed yourself dominating tier 100 nightmare dungeons, and still you're defeated by Hatred.
turns.zh: 也许我们都玩《暗黑破坏神》。也许《暗黑破坏神》被创造出来是为了看看一个德鲁伊，你的角色，能否最终击败超级莉莉丝。他们不知道。有趣的是，她的头衔是‘憎恨化身’。现在，我想你可以问问暗黑团队，但在永恒领域几乎不可能击败憎恨。你直播过自己碾压100层噩梦地下城，但仍然被憎恨击败。

turns.en: I can cruise through tier 100 nightmare dungeons like a stroll in the park, and still you're defeated by Hatred. The second hardest boss is Duriel; Duriel can't even scratch the paint. I've killed Duriel so many times, and every other boss in the game, all of them, killed them so many times, it's easy. But Uber Lilith, otherwise known as Hatred Incarnate, especially if you're a druid and you have no ability to go invulnerable, there are these random death waves that come at you. I'm 52, so my reflexes are not what they used to be, but I have a lifetime of playing video games. At one point I was maybe one of the best Quake players in the world. I actually won money for what I think was the first paid esports tournament in the US. We were doing four-person Quake tournaments, and we came second. I was the second best person on the team, and the actual best person, we were winning, we would have come first except his computer crashed halfway through the game. So we came second, but I got money for it. So basically I got skills, albeit no spring chicken these days. To be totally frank, it's driving me crazy trying to beat Lilith as a druid in the Eternal realm. This is really vexing. The challenge is part of the fun.
turns.zh: 我可以像逛公园一样轻松通过100层噩梦地下城，但仍然被憎恨击败。第二难的boss是督瑞尔；督瑞尔连我的漆都刮不掉。我杀了督瑞尔无数次，游戏里所有其他boss，全都杀了无数次，很容易。但超级莉莉丝，也就是‘憎恨化身’，尤其是如果你是个德鲁伊，没有无敌能力，会有这些随机死亡波向你袭来。我52岁了，反应不如从前，但我有玩一辈子游戏的经验。我曾经可能是世界上最好的《雷神之锤》玩家之一。我实际上在美国第一个付费电竞锦标赛中赢了钱。我们参加四人《雷神之锤》比赛，得了第二名。我是队里第二好的，而最好的那个，我们本来会赢，会得第一，但他的电脑在比赛途中崩溃了。所以我们得了第二，但我还是拿到了钱。所以基本上我有技术，尽管现在不再年轻了。坦率地说，在永恒领域用德鲁伊打败莉莉丝快把我逼疯了。这真的很烦人。挑战是乐趣的一部分。

The sorcerers have huge balls of lightning. In the seasonal realm, it's pretty easy to beat Uber Lilith because you get these vampiric powers that amplify your damage and increase your defense. turns.en: I like the idea that Elon Musk is fighting Hatred restlessly in this demonic realm. It's hilarious, absurd really. It makes me want to pull my hair out. What do you get from video games in general? For you personally?

turns.en: It calms my mind. Killing the demons in a video game calms the demons in my mind. If you play a tough video game, you can get into a state of flow, which is very enjoyable. So I expect that there will be very heavy usage of batteries in the future, because the peak-to-trough ratio for power plants is anywhere from 2 to 5, you know, so it's like lowest point to highest point. So batteries are necessary to balance it out.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得这很有帮助。物理学的工具非常强大，几乎可以应用于生活的任何领域。这其实就是批判性思维。对于重要的事情，你需要从第一性原理出发进行推理，并思考在某个方向上的极限。所以，在极限情况下，即使是在戴森球尺度，也就是即使你利用了整个太阳的能量，你仍然会关心每瓦有用算力。所以我认为这就是未来的方向。从AI的角度来看，我们现在面临硅短缺，大约一年后会转变为电压变压器短缺。讽刺的是，变压器用于Transformer——你需要变压器来运行Transformer。有人在这件事上很有幽默感。我觉得是的。哦，天哪，命运喜欢讽刺。讽刺的幽默和讽刺的有趣结果往往是命运想要的。幽默就是一切。我觉得香料就是一切。有人发过这个。是的，所以我们今天有硅短缺，大约一年后会有降压变压器短缺，然后大约两年后会出现普遍的电力短缺。我在一次公用事业公司和电力公司的全球聚会上发表过演讲，我说，你们真的需要为电力需求翻三倍做好准备，因为所有交通都将电气化，火箭是个讽刺的例外，供暖也将电气化。所以目前的能源使用，粗略来说，大约三分之一是电力，三分之一是交通，三分之一是供暖。所以为了让一切可持续发展，实现电气化，你需要将电力产出翻三倍。所以我鼓励公用事业公司建造更多发电厂，并且可能——不，不是可能，他们绝对应该购买更多电池，因为电网目前是为实时负载设计的，这有点疯狂，因为这意味着你必须根据峰值电力需求来设计，比如一年中最糟糕的秒或最糟糕的一天，否则就会发生电压下降或停电。我们在奥斯汀经历了持续几天的疯狂停电。因为电网几乎没有能量缓冲——如果你有水电站，你可以缓冲能量，但否则一切都是实时的。有了电池，你可以在夜间发电并在白天使用，所以你可以缓冲。所以我预计未来电池的使用会非常广泛，因为发电厂的峰谷比通常在2到5之间，也就是最低点到最高点。所以电池对于平衡是必要的。

turns.en: And then the demand, as you're saying, is going to grow, grow, grow, grow. Yeah, and part of that is the compute.
turns.zh: 然后需求，正如你所说，会不断增长。是的，其中一部分是算力。

turns.en: Yes, yes. I mean, electrification of transport and electric heating will be much bigger than AI, at least in the short term. But even for AI, you really have a growing demand for electricity for electric vehicles and a growing demand for electricity to run the computers for AI.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。我的意思是，交通电气化和电供暖在短期内将比AI大得多。但即使对于AI，电动汽车的电力需求也在增长，运行AI计算机的电力需求也在增长。

turns.en: Mhm. And so this is obviously leading, can lead to an electricity shortage. How difficult is the problem of, in this particular case, maximizing the useful productivity per watt for training? It seems like this is really where the big problem we're facing that needs to be solved is how to use the power efficiently. What you've learned so far about applying this physics first principle reasoning in this domain, how difficult is this problem?
turns.zh: 嗯。所以这显然会导致电力短缺。在这个特定案例中，最大化训练每瓦有用产出的问题有多难？似乎我们面临的真正需要解决的大问题是如何高效利用电力。你到目前为止在应用物理学第一性原理推理到这个领域方面学到了什么？这个问题有多难？

turns.en: It will get solved. It's the question of how long it takes to solve it. So at various points, there's a limit, some kind of limiting factor to progress. And with regard to scaling, right now the limiting factor is silicon chips. And that will—we're going to then have more chips than we can actually plug in and turn on, probably in about a year. The initial constraint being literally voltage step-down transformers, because you've got power coming in at 300,000 volts and it's got to step all the way down to eventually around 7 volts. So it's a very big voltage step-down, gigantic. And the industry is not used to rapid growth.
turns.zh: 这个问题会得到解决。只是需要多长时间的问题。所以在不同阶段，存在一个限制，某种进展的制约因素。关于Scaling（规模扩张），现在的限制因素是硅芯片。然后我们会有比实际能插电启动的更多的芯片，大概在一年内。最初的限制实际上是降压变压器，因为电力输入是30万伏，必须逐步降到最终大约7伏。所以这是一个非常大的降压，巨大的。而且这个行业不习惯快速增长。

transcript.sec: Competition and Safety in AGI Development

turns.en: Okay, let's talk about the competition here. You've shown concern about Google and Microsoft with OpenAI developing AGI. How can you help ensure with xAI and Tesla AI work that it doesn't become a competitive race to AGI, but instead is a collaborative development of safe AGI?
turns.zh: 好的，我们来谈谈竞争。你曾对谷歌和微软与OpenAI开发AGI表示担忧。你如何通过xAI和特斯拉AI的工作来确保这不会变成一场奔向AGI的竞争，而是协作开发安全的AGI？

turns.en: Well, I mean, I've been pushing for some kind of regulatory oversight for a long time. I've been somewhat of a Cassandra on the subject for over a decade. I think we want to be very careful in how we develop AI. It's a great power, and with great power comes great responsibility. I think it would be wise for us to have at least an objective third party who can be like a referee, that can go in and understand what the various leading players are doing with AI, and even if there's no enforcement ability, they can at least voice concerns publicly. You know, Jeff Hinton, for example, left Google and he voiced strong concerns, but now he's not at Google anymore, so who's going to voice the concerns? So I think there's—I like—you know, Tesla gets a lot of regulatory oversight on the automotive front. We're subject to, I think, over 100 regulatory agencies domestically and internationally. So it's a lot. You could fill this room with all the regulations that Tesla has to adhere to for automotive. Same is true for rockets and for—you know, currently the limiting factor for SpaceX for Starship launch is regulatory approval. The FAA has actually given their approval, but we're waiting for Fish and Wildlife to finish their analysis and give their approval. That's why I posted 'I want to buy a fish license,' which also refers to the Monty Python sketch. Yeah, like why do you need a license for your fish? I don't know. According to the rules, I'm told you need some sort of fish license or something. We effectively need a fish license to go to rocket, and I'm like, wait a second, how did the fish come into this picture? Yeah, I mean, some of the things like that that I feel are so absurd that I wanted like a comedy sketch, and flash at the bottom: 'This is all real, this is actually what happened.' You know, one of the things that was a bit of a challenge at one point is that they were worried about our rocket hitting a shark, and now the ocean's—
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，我长期以来一直在推动某种监管监督。十多年来，我有点像卡珊德拉。我认为我们在开发AI时需要非常小心。这是一种巨大的力量，能力越大，责任越大。我认为我们至少应该有一个客观的第三方，可以像裁判一样，去了解各个领先玩家在AI方面的动向，即使没有执法能力，他们至少可以公开表达担忧。你知道，例如杰夫·辛顿离开了谷歌，他表达了强烈的担忧，但现在他不在谷歌了，谁来表达担忧呢？所以我认为——我喜欢——你知道，特斯拉在汽车领域受到很多监管监督。我们受制于，我认为，国内外超过100个监管机构。所以很多。你可以用特斯拉必须遵守的所有汽车法规填满这个房间。火箭也是如此——你知道，目前SpaceX星舰发射的限制因素是监管批准。FAA实际上已经批准了，但我们正在等待鱼类和野生动物管理局完成他们的分析并给予批准。这就是为什么我发帖说‘我想买一个鱼许可证’，这也指的是巨蟒剧团的短剧。是的，就像为什么你需要给你的鱼办许可证？我不知道。根据规定，我被告知你需要某种鱼许可证之类的东西。我们实际上需要鱼许可证才能发射火箭，我就想，等等，鱼是怎么掺和进来的？是的，我的意思是，有些这样的事情我觉得太荒谬了，以至于我想要一个喜剧短剧，底部闪现：‘这都是真的，这实际上发生了。’你知道，曾经有一个挑战是，他们担心我们的火箭会撞到鲨鱼，现在海洋——

turns.en: Very big. And how often do you see sharks? Not that often. You know, as a percentage of ocean surface area, sharks basically go zero. And so then we said, well, how will we calculate the probability of hitting a shark? And they're like, well, we can't give you that information because they're worried about shark fin hunters going and hunting sharks. I said, well, how are we supposed to? We're on the horns of a dilemma then. Then they said, well, there's another part of fishing wildlife that can do this analysis. I'm like, well, why don't you give them the data? Like, we don't trust them. Like, excuse me, they're literally in your department. Again, this is actually what happened. And eventually they managed to solve the internal quandary, and indeed the probability of hitting a shark is essentially zero.
turns.zh: 非常大。你多久能看到鲨鱼？不常见。你知道，按海洋表面积百分比算，鲨鱼基本上为零。然后我们就说，那我们怎么计算撞到鲨鱼的概率？他们说，我们不能给你那个信息，因为他们担心鲨鱼鳍猎人会去捕鲨。我说，那我们该怎么办？我们陷入了两难。然后他们说，渔业野生动物部门有另一个部分可以做这个分析。我说，那你们为什么不把数据给他们？他们说，我们不信任他们。我说，不好意思，他们就在你们部门里。这确实是真事。最后他们解决了内部难题，确实撞到鲨鱼的概率基本为零。

turns.en: Then there's another organization that I didn't realize existed until a few months ago, that cares about whether we would potentially hit a whale in international waters. Now again, you look at the surface of the Pacific and say, what percentage of this consists of whale? Like, give you a big picture and point out all the whales in this picture. I'm like, I don't see any whales, like basically 0%. And if a rocket does hit a whale, which is extremely unlikely beyond all belief, that is the fate that has some seriously bad luck. You know, that's like the least lucky whale ever.
turns.zh: 然后还有另一个组织，我直到几个月前才知道它的存在，它关心我们是否可能在公海撞到鲸鱼。再看一遍，你看看太平洋表面，说这里面鲸鱼占多少百分比？比如给你一张大图，指出所有鲸鱼。我说，我没看到任何鲸鱼，基本是0%。如果火箭真的撞到鲸鱼，那概率极低，难以置信，那真是命运多舛，运气极差。你知道，那是最不幸的鲸鱼了。

turns.en: I mean, this is quite absurd bureaucracy. The bureaucracy of this, however, emerged. Yes. Well, I mean, one of the things that's pretty wild is, for launching out of Vandenberg in California, we had to worry about seal procreation, whether the seals would be distressed by the sonic booms. Now, there have been a lot of rockets launched out of Vandenberg, and the seal population has steadily increased. So if anything, rocket booms are an aphrodisiac, based on the evidence, if you were to correlate rocket launches with seal population. Nonetheless, we were forced to kidnap a seal, strap it to a board, put headphones on the seal, and play sonic boom sounds to it to see if it would be distressed. This is an actual thing that happened. This is actually real. I have pictures. Love to see this. Yeah. There, I mean, sorry, there's a seal with headphones. Yes, it's a seal with headphones strapped to a board. And the amazing part is how calm the seal was. Yeah, because if I was a seal, I'd be like, this is the end, they're definitely going to eat me. Yeah. How will the seal, when the seal goes back to other seal friends, how's it going to explain that? They're never going to believe him. Never going to believe it. That's why I'm like, well, you know, it's sort of like getting kidnapped by aliens and getting the anal probe. You know, you come back and say, I swear to God, I got kidnapped by aliens, they stuck an anal probe in my butt, and people are like, no they didn't, that's ridiculous. Seal buddies are never going to believe him that he got strapped to a board and they put headphones on his ears and then let him go. Twice, by the way. We had to do it twice. They let him go twice. We had to catch the same seal? Well, no, different seal. Okay. Did you get a seal of approval? Yeah, exactly. Approval. No, I mean, this is like, I don't think the public is quite aware of the madness that goes on. Yeah, it's absurd. Freaking seals with freaking headphones. I mean, this is a good encapsulation of the absurdity of human civilization. Seals and headphones. Yes.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，这真是荒谬的官僚主义。不过，这种官僚主义确实存在。是的。嗯，一件很疯狂的事是，从加州范登堡发射时，我们不得不担心海豹的繁殖问题，担心音爆会让海豹感到不安。现在，范登堡已经发射了很多火箭，海豹数量稳步增长。所以如果有什么关联的话，根据证据，火箭轰鸣反而是一种催情剂，如果你把火箭发射和海豹数量关联起来的话。尽管如此，我们被迫绑架了一只海豹，把它绑在板上，给它戴上耳机，播放音爆声音，看它是否会不安。这真的发生过。这是真的。我有照片。真想看看。是的。我是说，抱歉，有一只戴着耳机的海豹。是的，一只海豹戴着耳机被绑在板上。最神奇的是海豹有多冷静。是的，因为如果我是海豹，我会想，完蛋了，他们肯定要吃了我。是啊。当海豹回到其他海豹朋友那里时，它要怎么解释？它们永远不会相信它。永远不会相信。所以我就想，这有点像被外星人绑架然后被肛门探针。你知道，你回来说，我发誓，我被外星人绑架了，他们在我屁股里插了肛门探针，人们会说，不，他们没有，太荒谬了。海豹伙伴们永远不会相信它被绑在板上，耳朵上戴了耳机，然后被放走了。顺便说一句，两次。我们不得不做了两次。他们放了它两次。我们得抓同一只海豹吗？嗯，不，不同的海豹。好吧。你得到海豹的批准了吗？是的，没错。批准。不，我是说，我觉得公众不太了解这种疯狂。是啊，太荒谬了。该死的海豹戴着该死的耳机。我是说，这很好地概括了人类文明的荒谬。海豹和耳机。是的。

transcript.sec: Open sourcing AI and compute requirements

turns.en: What are the pros and cons of open sourcing AI to you, as another way to combat a company running away with AGI?
turns.zh: 对你来说，开源人工智能的利弊是什么，作为另一种防止公司独占AGI的方式？

turns.en: In order to run really deep intelligence, you need a lot of compute. So it's not like you can just fire up a PC in your basement and be running AGI, at least not yet. You know, Grok was trained on 8,000 A100s running at peak efficiency. And Grok's going to get a lot better, by the way. We'll be more than doubling our compute every couple months for the next several months. There's a nice write-up of how it went from Grok-0 to Grok-1. By Grok? Yeah, like Grok just bragging, making up about itself. Just Grok, Grok, Grok. Yeah, it's like a weird AI dating site exaggerates about itself. No, there's a write-up of where it stands now, the history of its development, and where it stands on some benchmarks compared to the state of the art, GPT-3.5. And so, I mean, there's Llama, you can open source once it's trained, you can open source a model, yeah, and for fine-tuning and all that kind of stuff. What do you think are the pros and cons of that, of open sourcing base models?
turns.zh: 要运行真正深度的智能，你需要大量算力。所以你不能只是在车库里启动一台PC就运行AGI，至少现在不行。你知道，Grok是在8000个A100上以峰值效率训练的。而且Grok会变得更好，顺便说一句。在接下来的几个月里，我们每几个月就会将算力翻倍以上。有一篇很好的文章介绍了它是如何从Grok-0发展到Grok-1的。是Grok写的吗？是的，就像Grok在自夸，编造关于自己的事。就是Grok、Grok、Grok。是的，就像一个奇怪的AI约会网站夸大自己。不，有一篇文章介绍了它现在的状况、开发历史，以及在一些基准测试中与最先进的GPT-3.5相比的表现。所以，我的意思是，有Llama，你可以在训练后开源，你可以开源一个模型，是的，用于微调之类的东西。你认为开源基础模型的利弊是什么？

turns.en: I think the smarter approach is to open source, I think perhaps with a slight time delay, you know, I don't know, six months even. I think I'm generally in favor of open sourcing, like bias towards open sourcing. I mean, it is a concern to me that, you know, OpenAI, I was arguably the prime mover behind OpenAI in the sense that it was created because of discussions that I had with Larry Page back when he and I were friends at his house, and I talked to him about AI safety, and Larry did not care about AI safety, at least at the time he didn't. You know, at one point he called me a speciesist for being pro-human, and I'm like, well, what team are you on, Larry? He's on team robot, I think. And I'm like, okay. So at the time, Google had acquired DeepMind, they had probably two-thirds of all AI researchers in the world. They had basically infinite money and compute, and the guy in charge, Larry Page, did not care about safety and even yelled at me and called me a speciesist for being pro-human.
turns.zh: 我认为更明智的做法是开源，也许有一点时间延迟，比如六个月。我总体上赞成开源，倾向于开源。我的意思是，我担心的是，OpenAI，我可以说我是OpenAI背后的主要推动者，因为它的创建源于我和拉里·佩奇的讨论，当时我们还是朋友，在他家，我和他谈到了AI安全，而拉里并不关心AI安全，至少当时不关心。你知道，有一次他称我为物种歧视者，因为我支持人类，我说，拉里，你站在哪一边？他站在机器人一边，我想。我说，好吧。当时，谷歌收购了DeepMind，他们可能拥有全世界三分之二的AI研究人员。他们基本上有无限的资金和算力，而负责人拉里·佩奇不关心安全，甚至对我大喊大叫，称我为物种歧视者，因为我支持人类。

turns.en: I don't know if you know about humans, they can change their mind, and maybe you and Larry Page can still be friends once more.
turns.zh: 我不知道你是否了解人类，他们可以改变主意，也许你和拉里·佩奇还能再做朋友。

turns.en: I'd like to be friends with Larry again. He got, the breaking of the friendship was over OpenAI. And specifically, I think the key moment was recruiting Ilya Sutskever. So I love Ilya, he's so brilliant. He's a good human, smart, good heart. And that was a tough recruiting battle. It was mostly Demis on one side and me on the other, both trying to recruit Ilya. And Ilya went back and forth, you know, was going to stay at Google, he was going to leave, then he was going to stay, then he leave. And finally he did agree to join OpenAI. That was one of the toughest recruiting battles we ever had. But that was really the linchpin for OpenAI being successful. And I was also instrumental in recruiting a number of other people, and I provided all the funding in the beginning, over $40 million. And the name, the 'Open' in OpenAI is supposed to be...
turns.zh: 我想再次和拉里做朋友。友谊的破裂是因为OpenAI。具体来说，我认为关键时刻是招募伊利亚·苏茨克维。我爱伊利亚，他非常聪明。他是个好人，聪明，心地善良。那是一场艰难的招聘战。主要是德米斯在一方，我在另一方，都想招募伊利亚。伊利亚犹豫不决，你知道，他打算留在谷歌，又打算离开，然后又留下，然后又离开。最后他同意加入OpenAI。那是我们经历过的最艰难的招聘战之一。但这确实是OpenAI成功的关键。我还帮助招募了其他一些人，并且我在一开始提供了所有资金，超过4000万美元。而名字，OpenAI中的'Open'应该是……

turns.en: mean open source and it was created as a nonprofit open source and now it is a closed source for maximum profit which I think is not good
turns.zh: 我的意思是开源，它最初是作为非营利开源创建的，现在却为了利润最大化而闭源，我认为这不好。

turns.zh: 我的意思是，X平台上的所有帖子都应该由AI推荐。我们应该为每个帖子填充一个向量空间，将其与每个用户的向量空间进行比较，然后匹配两者。目前推荐帖子只用了一点AI，大部分还是启发式方法。如果一条回复比原帖好得多，按照当前系统规则，它几乎得不到关注，远不如原帖。我了解到X的算法很多是开源的，也有相关文章，似乎有一些机器学习，但很分散，只有一点点。但它应该完全由AI驱动，至少对于你不关注的人的推荐应该全是AI。这是一个迷人的问题。首先，它从数亿条推文中选出1500条，这本身就很有趣，因为每天有数亿条帖子，它必须选出1500条，然后显然包括你关注的人，但还需要聚类来了解你是什么样的人，哪些新聚类可能与你相关。然后对这1500条进行排序和过滤，最终只推荐几条。最迷人的是速度：目前整个流程从数亿条到几条，单CPU需要220秒，但必须在1秒内完成，所以必须超级分布式。现在它不擅长推荐你不关注的人的内容，或者超过一度关系的内容。如果有关联性，比如你关注的人点赞、转发或评论了，效果还不错。但如果没有任何共同关注者，有人发了很有趣的内容，你也看不到。回复也基本不会被看到，因为它们的权重比原帖低几个数量级。你认为这能转化为端到端神经网络吗？是的，应该如此。推荐应该纯粹是向量相关性，有一系列向量，比如每个用户账户关联几百个向量，系统中的任何帖子，无论是视频、音频、短文还是长文——我之所以想摆脱“推文”这个词，是因为人们现在发两三个小时的视频，那不是推文。当只有140个字符时叫推文很合理，就像小鸟叽叽喳喳，但有了长内容就不再是推文了。电影不是推文。比如苹果在我们平台发布了《末日地堡》的整集，那是他们在所有平台上社交媒体互动最高的一次，是个好主意。我事后才知道，心想他们发了一整集，那不是推文，是视频。但从神经网络角度看，一切都很复杂，因为所有东西都是数据：一句聪明的笑话和三小时的视频在同一个池子里。目前这有点大杂烩。但比如苹果发布整集，会有很多讨论，你可以根据所有评论的上下文填充向量空间，即使它是视频，周围也有大量信息让你填充那个小时视频的向量空间，显然你可以得到更多。

turns.en: So Twitter has been instrumental in American politics and elections. What role do you think X will play in the 2024 US elections?
turns.zh: 那么Twitter在美国政治和选举中发挥了重要作用。你认为X在2024年美国大选中会扮演什么角色？

turns.en: Well, our goal is to be as even-handed and fair as possible. You know, whether someone is right, left, independent, whatever the case may be, that the platform is as fair and as much of a level playing field as possible. And in the past, Twitter has not been, because Twitter was controlled by far-left activists, objectively. They would describe themselves as that. So some people say, 'Well, how's it moved to the right?' Well, it's moved to the center. So from the perspective of the far left, yes, it has moved to the right, because everything's to the right from the far left. But no one on the far left that I'm aware of has been suspended or banned or de-amplified. So we're trying to be inclusive for the whole country and for other countries too. So there's a diversity of viewpoints. And free speech only matters if people you don't like are allowed to say things you don't like, because if that's not the case, you don't have free speech, and it's only a matter of time before the censorship is turned upon you.
turns.zh: 我们的目标是尽可能公平公正。无论一个人是右派、左派、独立派还是其他，平台都尽可能公平，提供公平的竞争环境。过去Twitter并非如此，因为客观上说，Twitter被极左活动家控制。他们自己也这么描述。所以有些人说'它怎么向右转了？'实际上它转向了中间。从极左的角度看，它确实向右转了，因为从极左看一切都是右的。但据我所知，没有任何极左人士被暂停、封禁或降权。所以我们努力包容整个国家以及其他国家。因此存在多样化的观点。言论自由只有在你不喜欢的人被允许说你不喜欢的话时才有意义，否则你就没有言论自由，审查迟早会落到你头上。

turns.en: The magic of Community notes is that it requires people who have historically disagreed in how they've rated notes to agree. To write or rate a note, you have to rate many notes. We use AI to populate a vector space around how someone has rated notes in the past. It's not as simple as left or right; life is much more complex. There are many correlations in how you rate Community notes posts. For a Community note to be shown, people who historically disagreed on a subject must agree. That's the essential magic of it.
turns.zh: 社区笔记的魔力在于，它要求历史上在笔记评分上存在分歧的人达成一致。要撰写或评分一条笔记，你必须先评分很多笔记。我们使用 AI 来构建一个向量空间，围绕一个人过去如何评分笔记。这并不像左派或右派那么简单；生活要复杂得多。你如何评分社区笔记帖子有很多相关性。要让一条社区笔记显示出来，历史上在该主题上存在分歧的人必须达成一致。这就是它的核心魔力。
